rawText:
Members of the Academy, members of the Philosophical Society:

What you have good reason to wish to hear from me today, what circumstances have perhaps qualified me to discuss with you on the basis of experience, is how to make atomic weapons. It is true, as we have so often and so earnestly said, that in the scientific studies which we had to carry out at Los Alamos in the practical arts there developed, there was little fundamental discovery. There was no great new insight into the nature of the physical world.

We had many surprises. We learned a good many things about atomic nuclei and many more about the behavior of matter under extreme and unfamiliar conditions, and not too few of the undertakings were in their quality and their style, worthy of the best traditions of physical science.

It would not be a dull story. It is being recorded in a great handbook of 15 volumes, much of which we think will be of interest to scientists, even if they are not by profession, makers of atomic bombs. It would be a pleasure to tell you a little about it. It would be a pleasure to help you to share our pride in the adequacy and the soundness of the physical science of our common heritage that went into this weapon that proved itself last summer in the New Mexico desert.

That would not be a dull story, but it is not one that I can tell today. It would be too dangerous to tell that story. That is what the President, on behalf of the people of the United States, has told us. That is what many of us, where we forced ourselves to make the decision, might well conclude.

What has come upon us, that the insight, the knowledge, the power of physical science, that the cultivation of which, to the learning and teaching of which we are dedicated, has become too dangerous to be talked of, even in these halls. It is that question that faces us now, that goes to the root of what science is, of what its value is. It is to that question to which tentatively, partially and with a profound sense of its difficulty and my own inadequacy I must try to speak today.

It is not a familiar question to us in these late days. It is not a familiar situation. If it seems to bear analogy to that raised by other weapons, to the need for certain secrecy, let us say, in the discussion of howitzers or torpedoes, that analogy will mislead us. There are some accidents in this situation, some things that may in the large light of history seem contingent.

Atomic weapons are based on things that are in the very frontier of physics. Their development is inextricably entangled with the growth of physics, as in all probability with that of the biological sciences and many practical arts. Atomic weapons were actually made by scientists; even some of you may think by scientists normally committed to the exploration of rather recondite things.

The speed of the development, the active and essential participation of men of science in the development, have no doubt contributed greatly to our awareness of the crisis that faces us, even to our sense of responsibility for its resolution. But these are contingent things.

What is not contingent is that we have made a thing, a most terrible weapon, that has altered abruptly and profoundly the nature of the world. We have made a thing that, by all standards of the world we grew up in, is an evil thing. By so doing, by our participation in making it possible to make these things, we have raised again the question of whether science is good for man, of whether it is good to learn about the world, to try to understand it, to try to control it, to help gift to the world of men increased insight, increased power.

Because we are scientists, we must say an unalterable "yes" to these questions. It is our faith and our commitment, seldom made explicit, even more seldom challenged that knowledge is a good in itself. Knowledge and such power as must come with it.

overview.title: Address to the American Philosophical Society
overview.source: American Philosophical Society, 1945 — J. Robert Oppenheimer

sentences.original: Members of the Academy, members of the Philosophical Society:
sentences.translation: 학술원 회원 여러분, 철학회 회원 여러분:

vocabulary.example: She was elected to the National Academy of Sciences.
They engaged in a philosophical debate about ethics.

expressions.usage: 공식 연설의 서두에서 청중을 격식 있게 호명할 때

patterns.pattern: Members of [A], members of [B]:
patterns.explanation: 격식 있는 연설의 전형적 오프닝. 청중을 소속 단체별로 호명하여 존중을 표현.
examples: Members of the Congress, members of the Senate:
Members of the faculty, members of the student body:

drillQuestions.question: Members _____ the Academy, members of the Philosophical Society.

sentences.notes: 격식체 연설의 전통적 시작법. 콜론(:)은 이후 본론이 시작됨을 알린다. "Philosophical Society"는 벤저민 프랭클린이 1743년에 설립한 미국 최초의 학술 단체인 미국철학회(American Philosophical Society)를 가리킨다.
sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 호격(apostrophe/direct address) — 격식 연설의 전통적 오프닝으로, 청중과의 공식적 관계를 설정

sentences.original: What you have good reason to wish to hear from me today, what circumstances have perhaps qualified me to discuss with you on the basis of experience, is how to make atomic weapons.
sentences.translation: 오늘 여러분이 저에게 듣고 싶어하시는 것, 어떤 상황이 아마도 저에게 경험에 기반하여 여러분과 논의할 자격을 부여한 것은, 원자 무기를 만드는 방법입니다.

grammarElements.text: What you have good reason to wish to hear from me today
grammarElements.label: 주어절 (what 명사절)

grammarElements.text: what circumstances have perhaps qualified me to discuss with you on the basis of experience
grammarElements.label: 주어절 (what 명사절 — 동격)

vocabulary.example: His experience qualifies him for the position.
The atomic age began in 1945.
Decisions were made on the basis of evidence.

expressions.usage: 상대방의 기대나 바람이 정당함을 인정할 때
자신의 권위나 전문성의 근거를 밝힐 때

patterns.pattern: What [you/they] have reason to [verb], what [noun] has [verb], is [complement]
patterns.explanation: what 명사절 두 개를 주어로 나열한 뒤, 하나의 보어로 연결하는 고급 문장 구조. 격식체 연설에서 기대를 정리한 뒤 핵심을 제시.
examples: What we hoped to achieve, what years of research had prepared us for, was a breakthrough.

drillQuestions.question: _____ you have good reason to wish to hear from me is how to make atomic weapons.
Circumstances have qualified me to discuss with you on the _____ of experience.

sentences.notes: 매우 길고 복잡한 문장. 두 개의 what 명사절이 주어로 병렬되며, 긴 수식어구 뒤에 핵심 보어("how to make atomic weapons")가 마지막에 등장하여 극적 효과를 만든다. 이는 오펜하이머의 특유의 문체.

koreanAlignment.en: What you have good reason to wish to hear from me today
koreanAlignment.ko: 오늘 여러분이 저에게 듣기를 바라는 것

koreanAlignment.en: what circumstances have perhaps qualified me to discuss with you
koreanAlignment.ko: 어떤 상황이 저에게 여러분과 논의할 자격을 부여한 것

koreanAlignment.en: is how to make atomic weapons

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 지연된 핵심(delayed thesis) — 긴 전제 뒤에 핵심어 "atomic weapons"를 배치하여 충격 효과 극대화

sentences.original: It is true, as we have so often and so earnestly said, that in the scientific studies which we had to carry out at Los Alamos in the practical arts there developed, there was little fundamental discovery.
sentences.translation: 우리가 그토록 자주 그리고 진지하게 말해왔듯이, 로스앨러모스에서 실용 기술 분야에서 수행해야 했던 과학적 연구에서, 근본적인 발견은 거의 없었다는 것은 사실입니다.

grammarElements.text: as we have so often and so earnestly said
grammarElements.label: 삽입절 (as — ~하듯이)

grammarElements.text: that in the scientific studies which we had to carry out at Los Alamos
grammarElements.label: 진주어 that절 (관계대명사 which 포함)

vocabulary.example: She earnestly pleaded for their support.
The team carried out a series of experiments.
This is a fundamental principle of physics.

expressions.usage: 어떤 사실을 인정하면서 논의를 시작할 때. 종종 but으로 반론이 이어짐

expressions.phrase: as we have so often said
expressions.usage: 이전에 반복된 발언을 참조할 때

patterns.pattern: It is true, as [we/they] have said, that [clause]
patterns.explanation: 가주어 it 구문에 삽입절(as...)을 끼워 넣은 구조. 이미 알려진 사실을 인정하면서 논의를 전개.
examples: It is true, as experts have warned, that climate change is accelerating.
It is true, as many have observed, that technology changes our behavior.

drillQuestions.question: It is true, _____ we have so often said, that there was little discovery.
There was _____ fundamental discovery in the work at Los Alamos.

sentences.notes: 가주어(It) ... 진주어(that절) 구조에 삽입절(as we have said)과 관계대명사절(which we had to carry out)이 중첩된 매우 복잡한 문장. "little"은 부정의 의미("거의 없는")로 사용. Los Alamos는 맨해튼 프로젝트의 핵심 연구소.

koreanAlignment.en: as we have so often and so earnestly said
koreanAlignment.ko: 우리가 그토록 자주 그리고 진지하게 말해왔듯이

koreanAlignment.en: that in the scientific studies which we had to carry out at Los Alamos

sentences.original: There was no great new insight into the nature of the physical world.
sentences.translation: 물리적 세계의 본질에 대한 위대한 새로운 통찰은 없었습니다.

grammarElements.text: into the nature of the physical world
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (방향/대상)

vocabulary.example: The study provided valuable insight into human behavior.
The nature of the problem is complex.

expressions.usage: 어떤 대상이나 현상에 대한 깊은 이해를 표현할 때
추상적 세계와 대비하여 물질적 현실을 가리킬 때

patterns.pattern: There was no [adjective] [noun] into [the nature of X]
patterns.explanation: 존재 구문의 부정형으로, 어떤 발견이나 진전이 없었음을 강조.
examples: There was no significant breakthrough into the cause of the disease.
There was no real progress into understanding the problem.

drillQuestions.question: There was no great new insight _____ the nature of the physical world.

sentences.notes: 앞 문장의 "little fundamental discovery"를 다른 표현으로 반복 강화. "insight into"에서 into는 "~에 대한"이라는 방향성을 나타낸다. 오펜하이머는 원자폭탄 개발이 순수 과학적 발견이 아닌 응용 기술이었음을 겸손하게 인정하고 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: into the nature of the physical world

sentences.original: We had many surprises.
sentences.translation: 우리에게는 많은 놀라움이 있었습니다.

vocabulary.example: The results came as a complete surprise.

expressions.usage: 예상치 못한 결과가 다수 있었음을 간결하게 표현

patterns.pattern: We had many [plural noun].
patterns.explanation: 간결한 전환 문장. 앞의 부정적 서술("little discovery") 뒤에 양보적으로 긍정적 측면을 소개.
examples: We had many challenges.
We had many breakthroughs along the way.

drillQuestions.question: We _____ many surprises during the research.

sentences.notes: 의도적으로 짧은 문장으로, 앞의 긴 복합문과 대비되어 리듬감을 만든다. "근본적 발견은 없었지만 놀라움은 많았다"라는 양보의 전환을 간결하게 표현.

sentences.original: We learned a good many things about atomic nuclei and many more about the behavior of matter under extreme and unfamiliar conditions, and not too few of the undertakings were in their quality and their style, worthy of the best traditions of physical science.
sentences.translation: 우리는 원자핵에 대해 꽤 많은 것을 배웠고, 극한적이고 생소한 조건에서의 물질의 행동에 대해서는 더 많이 배웠습니다. 그리고 적지 않은 수의 작업들이 그 질과 방식에 있어 물리학의 최고 전통에 부끄럽지 않은 것이었습니다.

grammarElements.text: a good many things about atomic nuclei
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (a good many = 꽤 많은)

grammarElements.text: and many more about the behavior of matter under extreme and unfamiliar conditions
grammarElements.label: 목적어 (병렬 — many more)

grammarElements.text: and not too few of the undertakings were ... worthy of the best traditions of physical science
grammarElements.label: 등위절 (이중부정 — not too few)

vocabulary.example: The nuclei of atoms contain protons and neutrons.
Building the bridge was a massive undertaking.
This achievement is worthy of recognition.

expressions.usage: "many"보다 강조된 표현으로, 상당한 수를 나타냄
겸손하면서도 성과를 인정하는 완곡한 표현

patterns.pattern: We learned [much] about [A] and many more about [B]
patterns.explanation: 두 분야에서 배운 것을 비교하며, 후자에서 더 많이 배웠음을 표현하는 패턴.
examples: We learned much about the symptoms and many more about the underlying causes.

drillQuestions.question: We learned a good _____ things about atomic nuclei.
Not too few of the undertakings were worthy _____ the best traditions.

sentences.notes: "a good many"는 "quite a lot of"의 격식체 표현. "not too few"는 이중부정(litotes)으로, "상당히 많은"이라는 뜻을 겸손하게 표현. nuclei는 라틴어 복수형으로, 단수형은 nucleus.

koreanAlignment.en: We learned a good many things
and many more about the behavior of matter
koreanAlignment.ko: 그리고 물질의 행동에 대해 더 많이

koreanAlignment.en: and not too few of the undertakings were
worthy of the best traditions of physical science
koreanAlignment.ko: 물리학의 최고 전통에 부끄럽지 않은 것이었습니다

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 이중부정(litotes) — "not too few"로 성과를 겸손하게 인정하는 수사법

sentences.original: It would not be a dull story.
sentences.translation: 그것은 지루한 이야기가 아닐 것입니다.

vocabulary.example: The lecture was rather dull.

expressions.usage: 가정법을 사용하여 실현 가능성을 열어두면서도 완곡하게 표현

patterns.pattern: It would not be a [adjective] [noun].
patterns.explanation: 이중부정(not + negative adjective)으로 긍정적 의미를 완곡하게 전달. "재미있을 것이다"를 직접 말하지 않는 영국식 겸양.
examples: It would not be an uninteresting experiment.
It would not be a wasted effort.

drillQuestions.question: It _____ not be a dull story.

sentences.notes: "would not be a dull story"는 "it would be an exciting story"를 이중부정(litotes)으로 표현. 오펜하이머가 자신의 경험을 이야기하고 싶지만 할 수 없다는 아이러니를 준비하는 문장.

sentences.original: It is being recorded in a great handbook of 15 volumes, much of which we think will be of interest to scientists, even if they are not by profession, makers of atomic bombs.
sentences.translation: 그것은 15권의 방대한 편람에 기록되고 있으며, 그 대부분이 과학자들에게 흥미로울 것이라고 생각합니다. 비록 그들이 직업상 원자폭탄 제조자가 아니더라도 말입니다.

grammarElements.text: in a great handbook of 15 volumes
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (장소/매체)

grammarElements.text: much of which we think will be of interest to scientists
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (much of which)

grammarElements.text: even if they are not by profession, makers of atomic bombs
grammarElements.label: 양보절 (even if)

vocabulary.example: The employee handbook outlines company policies.
The encyclopedia consists of 20 volumes.
She is a lawyer by profession.

expressions.usage: "interesting"보다 격식 있는 표현
양보를 나타내는 접속사

patterns.pattern: [noun], much of which [we think] will be of interest to [audience]
patterns.explanation: 관계대명사 which 앞에 "much of"를 사용하여 부분을 지시하는 고급 구조.
examples: The report, much of which remains classified, was submitted to Congress.

drillQuestions.question: It is being recorded in a great handbook, much of _____ will be of interest.
They are not _____ profession, makers of atomic bombs.

sentences.notes: "is being recorded"는 현재진행 수동태로, 기록 작업이 진행 중임을 나타낸다. "much of which"는 비제한적 관계대명사절로, 선행사(handbook)의 내용 중 상당 부분을 지시. "by profession"은 동격 구조로 "makers of atomic bombs"를 수식.

koreanAlignment.en: in a great handbook of 15 volumes
much of which we think will be of interest to scientists
koreanAlignment.ko: 그 대부분이 과학자들에게 흥미로울 것이라 생각합니다

koreanAlignment.en: even if they are not by profession

sentences.original: It would be a pleasure to tell you a little about it.
sentences.translation: 그것에 대해 조금 이야기해드리는 것은 기쁜 일일 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: to tell you a little about it
grammarElements.label: 진주어 (to부정사)

vocabulary.example: It was a pleasure to meet you.

expressions.phrase: it would be a pleasure to
expressions.usage: 격식체에서 무언가를 하고 싶은 의향을 정중하게 표현
겸손하게 이야기의 범위를 제한하는 표현

patterns.pattern: It would be a pleasure to [verb] ...
patterns.explanation: 가주어 구문과 가정법을 결합한 격식체 표현. 실현되지 않는 소망을 정중하게 표현.
examples: It would be a pleasure to share our findings with the committee.
It would be a pleasure to host you at our institution.

drillQuestions.question: It would be a _____ to tell you about our work.

sentences.notes: 가주어(It) + would be + 보어(a pleasure) + 진주어(to tell...) 구조. 이 문장과 다음 문장에서 "It would be a pleasure"가 반복되며, 이후 "할 수 없다"는 반전으로 이어지는 수사학적 장치를 준비.

koreanAlignment.en: to tell you a little about it

sentences.original: It would be a pleasure to help you to share our pride in the adequacy and the soundness of the physical science of our common heritage that went into this weapon that proved itself last summer in the New Mexico desert.
sentences.translation: 우리의 공동 유산인 물리학의 적절함과 건전함에 대한 자부심을 여러분과 나누도록 돕는 것은 기쁜 일일 것입니다. 그 물리학이 지난여름 뉴멕시코 사막에서 스스로를 증명한 이 무기에 들어갔으니 말입니다.

grammarElements.text: to help you to share our pride in the adequacy and the soundness of the physical science
grammarElements.label: 진주어 (to부정사 + 전치사구 중첩)

grammarElements.text: that proved itself last summer in the New Mexico desert
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (weapon을 수식)

vocabulary.example: The adequacy of the safety measures was questioned.
The soundness of the argument was widely accepted.
We must preserve our cultural heritage.

expressions.usage: 과학계 전체가 공유하는 지적 전통을 가리킬 때
이론이나 기술이 실제로 효과가 있음을 입증했을 때

patterns.pattern: our pride in the [quality] of [the science] that went into [this result]
patterns.explanation: 중첩된 전치사구(pride in → adequacy of → science of → heritage that)로 논리적 관계를 세밀하게 표현.
examples: Our confidence in the reliability of the engineering that went into this bridge.

drillQuestions.question: It would be a pleasure to help you share our pride _____ the adequacy of the science.
The weapon _____ itself last summer in the desert.

sentences.notes: 매우 긴 단일 문장으로, 전치사구가 4단계로 중첩(pride in → adequacy/soundness of → science of → heritage). "proved itself last summer"는 1945년 7월 16일 뉴멕시코 트리니티 핵실험을 가리킨다. 이 문장은 과학적 자부심과 곧 올 "하지만 말할 수 없다"의 반전 사이의 긴장감을 고조시킨다.

koreanAlignment.en: It would be a pleasure to help you
koreanAlignment.ko: 여러분을 돕는 것은 기쁜 일일 것입니다

koreanAlignment.en: of the physical science of our common heritage
that proved itself last summer in the New Mexico desert

sentences.original: That would not be a dull story, but it is not one that I can tell today.
sentences.translation: 그것은 지루한 이야기가 아닐 것입니다만, 오늘 제가 할 수 있는 이야기는 아닙니다.

vocabulary.example: Life is never dull with her around.

expressions.usage: 능력이나 허가의 부재를 정중하게 표현

patterns.pattern: [That] would not be [X], but it is not one that [I can verb]
patterns.explanation: 양보(would not be X) 뒤에 역접(but)으로 제한을 두는 패턴. 아쉬움과 불가능을 동시에 표현.
examples: That would not be an easy task, but it is not one that we can avoid.

drillQuestions.question: It is not _____ that I can tell today.

sentences.notes: 앞서 반복했던 "It would not be a dull story"를 "That"로 바꿔 다시 반복한 뒤, "but it is not one that I can tell today"로 극적 반전. 과학적 비밀유지(secrecy)의 딜레마를 표현하는 핵심 전환점.

koreanAlignment.en: That would not be a dull story

koreanAlignment.ko: 오늘 제가 할 수 있는 이야기

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 반복과 반전(repetition with reversal) — "not a dull story"를 세 번째로 반복한 뒤 "but" 이하에서 결정적 반전

sentences.original: It would be too dangerous to tell that story.
sentences.translation: 그 이야기를 하는 것은 너무 위험할 것입니다.

vocabulary.example: It is dangerous to drive in icy conditions.

expressions.usage: too + 형용사 + to부정사 구조로 불가능한 이유를 제시

patterns.pattern: It would be too [adjective] to [verb].
patterns.explanation: too...to 구문과 가정법을 결합. "너무 ~해서 ~할 수 없다"라는 불가능성을 완곡하게 표현.
examples: It would be too risky to proceed without approval.
It would be too costly to rebuild from scratch.

drillQuestions.question: It would be _____ dangerous to tell that story.

sentences.notes: 짧고 단호한 문장. "too dangerous"라는 표현은 오펜하이머가 핵무기의 과학적 세부사항을 공개할 수 없는 이유를 한마디로 요약. 지식 자체가 위험해진 시대의 역설을 암시.

sentences.original: That is what the President, on behalf of the people of the United States, has told us.
sentences.translation: 그것이 대통령이 미국 국민을 대신하여 우리에게 말한 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: what the President has told us
grammarElements.label: 보어 (what 명사절)

grammarElements.text: on behalf of the people of the United States
grammarElements.label: 삽입구 (전치사구 — 대리/대표)

vocabulary.example: I am speaking on behalf of the entire team.
The President addressed the nation.

expressions.phrase: that is what [someone] has told us
expressions.usage: 권위 있는 출처의 발언을 인용할 때
공식적 권한의 근거를 밝힐 때

patterns.pattern: That is what [authority], on behalf of [group], has [past participle]
patterns.explanation: 권위를 빌려 정당성을 부여하는 패턴. 삽입구가 권한의 범위를 명시.
examples: That is what the committee, on behalf of the shareholders, has decided.

drillQuestions.question: The President spoke on _____ of the people of the United States.

sentences.notes: "on behalf of"는 격식체에서 대리/대표를 나타내는 핵심 표현. 여기서 오펜하이머는 비밀유지 의무가 자신의 선택이 아닌 대통령(트루먼)의 명령임을 밝히고 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: on behalf of the people of the United States

sentences.original: That is what many of us, where we forced ourselves to make the decision, might well conclude.
sentences.translation: 그것은 우리 중 많은 사람들이, 스스로 결정을 내리도록 강제한 경우에, 충분히 결론지을 수 있는 것이기도 합니다.

grammarElements.text: what many of us might well conclude
grammarElements.label: 보어 (what 명사절)

grammarElements.text: where we forced ourselves to make the decision
grammarElements.label: 삽입절 (조건/상황)

vocabulary.example: The investigators concluded that the fire was accidental.
She forced herself to stay calm during the crisis.

expressions.usage: "might well"은 가능성이 상당히 높음을 나타내는 표현

expressions.phrase: forced ourselves to make the decision
expressions.usage: 힘든 결정을 자발적으로 내렸음을 표현

patterns.pattern: That is what [many of us] might well [verb]
patterns.explanation: "might well"은 조동사 might에 well을 더해 "충분히 ~할 수 있다"라는 강화된 가능성을 표현.
examples: That is what any reasonable person might well believe.
That is what many experts might well recommend.

drillQuestions.question: Many of us might _____ conclude the same thing.
We forced _____ to make the decision.

sentences.notes: "might well"은 "~할 만하다, 충분히 ~할 수 있다"라는 뜻으로, 단순 might보다 가능성이 높음을 나타낸다. 오펜하이머는 비밀유지가 대통령의 명령일 뿐만 아니라 과학자들 스스로도 동의할 수 있는 결론임을 인정하고 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: where we forced ourselves to make the decision

sentences.original: What has come upon us, that the insight, the knowledge, the power of physical science, that the cultivation of which, to the learning and teaching of which we are dedicated, has become too dangerous to be talked of, even in these halls.
sentences.translation: 우리에게 닥친 것은, 물리학의 통찰과 지식과 힘, 우리가 헌신하는 그것의 배양과 학습과 교육이, 이 강당에서조차 이야기하기에 너무 위험해졌다는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: that the insight, the knowledge, the power of physical science
grammarElements.label: 동격절 (that — 설명)

grammarElements.text: that the cultivation of which, to the learning and teaching of which we are dedicated
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (of which — 중첩)

grammarElements.text: has become too dangerous to be talked of
grammarElements.label: 동사 (현재완료 + too...to)

vocabulary.example: A strange feeling came upon her.
The cultivation of knowledge requires patience.
She is dedicated to her research.

expressions.usage: 피할 수 없는 상황이 도래했음을 표현

expressions.phrase: too dangerous to be talked of
expressions.usage: too...to 구문의 수동태 형태
학문의 전당에서조차 제한이 있다는 아이러니를 강조

patterns.pattern: What has come upon us [is] that [X] has become too [adjective] to be [past participle]
patterns.explanation: 우리에게 닥친 상황이 무엇인지 설명하는 구조. "too...to"와 수동태가 결합.
examples: What has come upon the industry is that innovation has become too expensive to be sustained.

drillQuestions.question: What has come _____ us is a profound crisis.
Physical science has become too dangerous to be talked _____.

sentences.notes: 오펜하이머 연설에서 가장 복잡하고 감동적인 문장 중 하나. 여러 겹의 관계절이 중첩되어 문장 구조가 매우 난해하다. "even in these halls"는 학문의 자유로운 공간에서조차 과학 지식을 논의할 수 없게 되었다는 깊은 아이러니와 비탄을 담고 있다.

koreanAlignment.en: that the insight, the knowledge, the power of physical science
the cultivation of which, to the learning and teaching of which we are dedicated
has become too dangerous to be talked of

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 열거법(enumeration) + 아이러니(irony) — 과학의 가치를 열거한 뒤, 그것이 "위험해졌다"는 역설적 결론

sentences.original: It is that question that faces us now, that goes to the root of what science is, of what its value is.
sentences.translation: 바로 그 질문이 지금 우리에게 직면한 것이며, 과학이 무엇인지, 그 가치가 무엇인지의 근본에 닿는 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: that goes to the root of what science is
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (병렬)

vocabulary.example: We face many challenges in the coming years.
We need to get to the root of the problem.

expressions.usage: 문제의 본질이나 핵심에 도달함을 표현
근본적 정의에 대한 질문을 제기할 때

patterns.pattern: It is [that X] that [verb A], that [verb B]
patterns.explanation: 분열문(It is ... that)으로 특정 요소를 강조한 뒤, 관계대명사절을 병렬로 이어 설명을 확장.
examples: It is this principle that guides us, that shapes our decisions.

drillQuestions.question: It is that question that goes to the _____ of what science is.
That question _____ us now.

sentences.notes: "It is ... that" 분열문(cleft sentence)으로 "that question"을 강조. 관계대명사 that이 세 번 다른 역할로 사용: (1) 분열문의 that, (2) faces의 주격 관계대명사, (3) goes의 주격 관계대명사.

koreanAlignment.en: that goes to the root of
what science is, of what its value is

sentences.original: It is to that question to which tentatively, partially and with a profound sense of its difficulty and my own inadequacy I must try to speak today.
sentences.translation: 바로 그 질문에 대해, 조심스럽게, 부분적으로, 그리고 그 어려움과 저 자신의 부족함에 대한 깊은 인식을 가지고, 오늘 말씀드려 보아야 합니다.

grammarElements.text: tentatively, partially and with a profound sense of its difficulty and my own inadequacy
grammarElements.label: 부사구 (열거 — 3개의 수식어)

grammarElements.text: I must try to speak today
grammarElements.label: 주절

vocabulary.example: She tentatively raised her hand to ask a question.
The experience had a profound impact on her life.
He struggled with feelings of inadequacy.

expressions.usage: 깊이 있는 자각이나 감정을 표현할 때
겸손하게 자신의 한계를 인정할 때

patterns.pattern: It is to [that question] to which [adverbially modified] I must try to [verb]
patterns.explanation: 분열문으로 대상을 강조하고, 긴 부사구로 태도의 겸양을 표현한 뒤 주절을 이어가는 고급 구조.
examples: It is to that challenge to which humbly and with great caution we must respond.

drillQuestions.question: I must try to speak with a profound sense of its difficulty and my own _____.
_____, partially and with a profound sense, I must try to speak.

sentences.notes: 분열문(It is ... to which)과 긴 부사적 수식어가 결합된 매우 정교한 문장. "tentatively, partially"의 부사 나열과 "with a profound sense of ..."의 전치사구가 오펜하이머의 겸손함과 주제의 무거움을 동시에 전달. 지적 겸양(intellectual humility)의 모범적 표현.

koreanAlignment.en: and with a profound sense of its difficulty
koreanAlignment.ko: 그리고 그 어려움에 대한 깊은 인식을 가지고

koreanAlignment.en: I must try to speak today

sentences.rhetoricalDevice: 겸양(captatio benevolentiae) — 화자의 부족함을 인정하여 청중의 호의를 구하는 고전적 수사법

sentences.original: It is not a familiar question to us in these late days.
sentences.translation: 이 늦은 시대에 우리에게 익숙한 질문이 아닙니다.

vocabulary.example: This concept is familiar to most students.
In these late days of the empire, decline was inevitable.

expressions.usage: 문명이 성숙했거나 위기에 처한 시대를 지칭하는 문학적 표현

patterns.pattern: It is not a familiar [noun] to us in these [adjective] days.
patterns.explanation: 현재의 낯선 상황을 강조하는 구조. "not familiar"은 전례 없는 도전을 암시.
examples: It is not a familiar dilemma to us in these modern times.

drillQuestions.question: It is not a familiar question _____ us in these late days.

sentences.notes: "these late days"는 "이 말기적 시대"라는 뉘앙스를 가진 문학적 표현. 과학이 인류에게 해가 될 수 있는지라는 질문이 근대 이후 처음 진지하게 제기되고 있음을 암시.

koreanAlignment.en: It is not a familiar question

sentences.original: If it seems to bear analogy to that raised by other weapons, to the need for certain secrecy, let us say, in the discussion of howitzers or torpedoes, that analogy will mislead us.
sentences.translation: 만약 그것이 다른 무기들이 제기한 문제와, 예를 들어 곡사포나 어뢰에 대한 논의에서의 특정 비밀유지의 필요성과 유사해 보인다면, 그 유추는 우리를 오도할 것입니다.

grammarElements.text: If it seems to bear analogy to that raised by other weapons
grammarElements.label: 조건절 (if)

grammarElements.text: to the need for certain secrecy
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (병렬 — to)

grammarElements.text: in the discussion of howitzers or torpedoes
grammarElements.label: 전치사구 (구체적 예시)

vocabulary.example: He drew an analogy between the brain and a computer.
The project was carried out in great secrecy.
Statistics can mislead if not properly interpreted.
The artillery unit deployed howitzers on the hill.
The submarine launched a torpedo at the enemy ship.

expressions.usage: 두 상황 사이의 유사점을 지적할 때. "draw an analogy"보다 격식적
구체적 예시를 완곡하게 도입할 때

patterns.pattern: If it seems to bear analogy to [X], that analogy will mislead us.
patterns.explanation: 조건절에서 유추를 인정한 뒤, 주절에서 그 유추가 잘못되었음을 경고하는 논증 패턴.
examples: If this seems to bear analogy to past crises, that analogy will deceive us.

drillQuestions.question: If it seems to bear _____ to other weapons, that will mislead us.
That analogy will _____ us if we are not careful.

sentences.notes: "bear analogy"는 "유추를 지니다"라는 격식체 표현. 오펜하이머는 원자 무기가 곡사포나 어뢰와 같은 기존 무기와 근본적으로 다르다고 주장. "let us say"는 삽입구로, 구체적 예시를 완곡하게 제시.

koreanAlignment.en: If it seems to bear analogy
to that raised by other weapons
to the need for certain secrecy
let us say, in the discussion of howitzers or torpedoes
koreanAlignment.ko: 예를 들어 곡사포나 어뢰에 대한 논의에서의

sentences.original: Atomic weapons are based on things that are in the very frontier of physics.
sentences.translation: 원자 무기는 물리학의 최전선에 있는 것들에 기반합니다.

grammarElements.text: that are in the very frontier of physics
grammarElements.label: 관계대명사절 (things를 수식)

vocabulary.example: AI is at the frontier of modern technology.
The movie is based on a true story.

expressions.usage: "very"가 강조 부사로 "바로 그"의 의미. 가장 앞선 영역을 표현

patterns.pattern: [X] are based on things that are in the very frontier of [field]
patterns.explanation: 어떤 것이 최첨단 분야에 기반하고 있음을 설명하는 패턴. "very"가 "바로 그"라는 강조 역할.
examples: Modern medicine is based on things that are in the very frontier of genetics.
Quantum computing is based on things at the very frontier of physics.

drillQuestions.question: Atomic weapons are based _____ things at the frontier of physics.
They are in the _____ frontier of physics.

sentences.notes: "the very frontier"에서 very는 형용사로 "바로 그, 정확히"라는 강조의 의미. 원자 무기가 단순한 기술이 아니라 물리학의 가장 앞선 지식에 기반한다는 점을 강조하여, 무기 개발과 순수 과학의 불가분성을 주장하는 근거를 마련.

koreanAlignment.en: that are in the very frontier of physics
